questions.question:
Read the statements:
- All women are entrepreneurs.
- Some women are doctors.

Which of the following conclusions can be logically inferred from the above statements?

A. All women are doctors
B. All doctors are entrepreneurs
C. All entrepreneurs are women
D. Some entrepreneurs are doctors
questions.to_find: Which conclusion is logically inferred from the given categorical statements

understand.plain: We have two categorical propositions: a universal affirmative (All women are entrepreneurs) and a particular affirmative (Some women are doctors). Using syllogistic reasoning, we need to find the conclusion that necessarily follows. The key insight is that if some women are doctors and all women are entrepreneurs, then those same women who are doctors are also entrepreneurs, making some entrepreneurs doctors.

keywords.explain: A statement of the form 'All A are B' meaning every member of A is also a member of B.
keywords.example: 'All women are entrepreneurs' means every woman belongs to the entrepreneur category.

keywords.explain: A statement of the form 'Some A are B' meaning at least one member of A is also a member of B.
keywords.example: 'Some women are doctors' means there exists at least one woman who is a doctor.

keywords.explain: Drawing a valid conclusion from two premises by tracking the shared (middle) term between them.
keywords.example: If some women (S) are doctors (P), and all women (S) are entrepreneurs (M), then some entrepreneurs (M) are doctors (P).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='340' height='200' viewBox='0 0 340 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='200' rx='12' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><ellipse cx='130' cy='110' rx='100' ry='65' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5'/><ellipse cx='160' cy='115' rx='45' ry='35' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7'/><text x='55' y='105' class='sub ink'>Entrepreneurs</text><text x='100' y='125' class='sub ink'>Women</text><text x='140' y='118' class='sub ink' font-size='12'>Doctors</text><text x='170' y='40' class='tag ink'>Some entrepreneurs = doctors</text><line x1='170' y1='45' x2='165' y2='108' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram showing Women as a subset of Entrepreneurs, with Doctors overlapping Women, illustrating that some entrepreneurs are doctors.

given.aim: Find which conclusion is logically valid given the two categorical premises

terms.meaning: Women set is entirely contained within the entrepreneurs set (W subset E).
terms.example: Every woman you name is necessarily an entrepreneur.
terms.connects: This allows us to substitute 'entrepreneur' for 'woman' when moving information upward.

terms.meaning: The intersection of women and doctors is non-empty (W intersect D != empty set).
terms.example: At least one woman is a doctor.
terms.connects: Combined with Premise 1, these women who are doctors are also entrepreneurs.

terms.meaning: The term appearing in both premises that links entrepreneurs and doctors.
terms.example: Women appear in both statements, serving as the bridge in the syllogism.
terms.connects: Eliminating the middle term yields the valid conclusion about the relationship between entrepreneurs and doctors.

given.plan: Use Venn diagram reasoning: since women subset entrepreneurs, any woman who is a doctor is also an entrepreneur. Since some women are doctors, some entrepreneurs are doctors.

steps.title: Translate premises to set notation
steps.formula_raw: 'All A have property B' = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: Premise 1: forall x (Woman(x) -> Entrepreneur(x)). Premise 2: exists x (Woman(x) AND Doctor(x)).
steps.note: The universal premise tells us W is a subset of E; the particular premise tells us W and D overlap.

steps.title: Apply syllogistic reasoning to derive the conclusion
steps.formula_raw: 'Some A has property B' = (exists x)(A(x) and B(x))
steps.apply: From Premise 2: exists x such that Woman(x) AND Doctor(x). By Premise 1, this same x satisfies Entrepreneur(x). Therefore: exists x (Entrepreneur(x) AND Doctor(x)), i.e., Some entrepreneurs are doctors.
steps.note: The conclusion 'Some entrepreneurs are doctors' necessarily follows by substitution through the universal premise.

steps.title: Verify the other options are not valid inferences
steps.formula_raw: Statement R implies C when every model making R true also makes C true
steps.apply: A: 'All women are doctors' - not supported (Premise 2 only says SOME). B: 'All doctors are entrepreneurs' - not inferable (doctors outside the women set are unspecified). C: 'All entrepreneurs are women' - this is the converse of Premise 1, which does not follow logically.
steps.note: Only option D necessarily follows from the given premises.

questions.formula_note: Categorical syllogism: a universal (All A are B) combined with a particular (Some A are C) yields a particular conclusion (Some B are C) by substituting through the shared middle term.

questions.question:
Humpty Dumpty sits on a wall every day while having lunch. The wall sometimes breaks. A person sitting on the wall falls if the wall breaks.

Which one of the statements below is logically valid and can be inferred from the above sentences?

A. Humpty Dumpty always falls while having lunch
B. Humpty Dumpty sometimes never falls while having lunch
C. Humpty Dumpty never falls during dinner
D. When Humpty Dumpty does not sit on the wall, the wall does not break
questions.answer: B. Humpty Dumpty sometimes never falls while having lunch
questions.to_find: Which statement is logically inferable from the three given conditional premises

understand.plain: We have three facts: (1) Humpty sits on the wall every day at lunch, (2) the wall sometimes breaks, and (3) if the wall breaks and someone sits on it, they fall. Since the wall only SOMETIMES breaks, Humpty falls only when the wall breaks. On the days when the wall does NOT break, Humpty does not fall. So it is valid to say Humpty sometimes (on non-break days) never falls during lunch.

keywords.explain: A statement 'If A then B' only guarantees B when A is true; when A is false, B may or may not be true.
keywords.example: 'A person falls IF the wall breaks' - falling is only guaranteed when the wall actually breaks.

keywords.explain: 'Sometimes' means there exist occasions; 'always' means on every occasion without exception.
keywords.example: The wall sometimes breaks, not always. So there are days when Humpty sits on the wall but it does NOT break.

keywords.explain: From 'If P then Q' and P being true, we may conclude Q.
keywords.example: Wall breaks AND person is sitting => person falls. Wall does NOT break => we cannot conclude the person falls.

understand.visual_alt: Timeline showing lunch days: some days the wall breaks and Humpty falls, other days the wall does not break and Humpty does not fall.

given.aim: Identify the logically valid inference from the three premises about Humpty Dumpty

terms.term: Premise 1: Humpty sits on wall every day at lunch
terms.example: Each day during lunch Humpty is always on the wall.
terms.connects: This ensures the condition 'sitting on the wall' is always satisfied during lunch.

terms.meaning: exists d (LunchDay(d) AND WallBreaks(d)), but NOT forall d WallBreaks(d)
terms.example: Some days the wall breaks, other days it remains intact.
terms.connects: Since it is not always breaking, there are days when Humpty sits on an intact wall and does NOT fall.

terms.term: Premise 3: A person sitting on wall falls if wall breaks
terms.meaning: forall d (SittingOnWall(Person, d) AND WallBreaks(d) -> Falls(Person, d))
terms.example: Falling is contingent on both conditions: being on the wall AND the wall breaking.
terms.connects: Combined with Premise 1, Humpty falls on days when the wall breaks, but not necessarily on other days.

given.plan: Enumerate the two scenarios: wall breaks (Humpty falls) and wall does not break (Humpty does not necessarily fall). Check each option against both scenarios.

steps.title: Analyse the scenario when wall breaks
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and A, conclude B
steps.apply: On days when the wall breaks: SittingOnWall(Humpty) AND WallBreaks => Falls(Humpty). So Humpty falls on those days.
steps.note: This is the direct application of Premise 3 with Premise 1.

steps.title: Analyse the scenario when wall does NOT break
steps.formula_raw: p -> q is False only when p is True and q is False
steps.apply: When wall does NOT break, the antecedent of Premise 3 is false, so we CANNOT conclude Humpty falls. Humpty is still on the wall but does not fall.
steps.note: Since the wall only sometimes breaks (Premise 2), there exist days when Humpty sits on the wall without falling.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (not A), you may NOT conclude (not B)
steps.apply: A: 'Always falls' - FALSE, wall only sometimes breaks so Humpty does not always fall. B: 'Sometimes never falls during lunch' - TRUE, on days when wall does not break, Humpty sits on intact wall and does not fall. C: 'Never falls during dinner' - Not inferable; dinner is unspecified. D: 'Wall does not break when Humpty is not sitting' - Cannot infer this, it is the fallacy of denying the antecedent.
steps.note: Only option B correctly captures the partial nature: some lunch days the wall stays intact and Humpty does not fall.

solution.result: B. Humpty Dumpty sometimes never falls while having lunch

questions.formula_note: A conditional 'falls if wall breaks' only guarantees falling when the wall does break; since the wall only sometimes breaks, falling is not universal - Humpty sometimes (on days the wall is intact) does not fall during lunch.

questions.question:
Given below are two statements followed by two conclusions. Assuming these statements to be true, decide which one logically follows.

Statements:
I. All film stars are playback singers.
II. All film directors are film stars.

Conclusions:
I. All film directors are playback singers.
II. Some film stars are film directors.

A. Only conclusion I follows.
B. Only conclusion II follows.
C. Neither conclusion I nor II follows.
D. Both conclusions I and II follow.
questions.answer: D. Both conclusions I and II follow.
questions.to_find: Which of the two conclusions logically follow from the given pair of categorical statements

understand.plain: We are given two universal affirmative statements forming a syllogism chain. Statement II tells us film directors are a subset of film stars, and Statement I tells us film stars are a subset of playback singers. By transitivity, film directors are a subset of playback singers (Conclusion I). Also, since all film directors are film stars, there exist film stars who are film directors (Conclusion II follows from the subset relationship).

keywords.explain: If set A is a subset of B and B is a subset of C, then A is a subset of C.
keywords.example: Directors subset Stars, Stars subset Singers => Directors subset Singers.

keywords.explain: If all A are B (non-empty A), then some B are A. The converse of a universal gives a particular.
keywords.example: 'All film directors are film stars' implies 'Some film stars are film directors' (assuming directors exist).

keywords.explain: The classic AAA-1 syllogism: All M are P, All S are M, therefore All S are P.
keywords.example: All film stars are singers (M->P), All directors are film stars (S->M), so All directors are singers (S->P).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='340' height='210' viewBox='0 0 340 210'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='210' rx='12' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><ellipse cx='170' cy='120' rx='140' ry='75' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='165' cy='125' rx='90' ry='50' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.5'/><ellipse cx='162' cy='130' rx='45' ry='28' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.85'/><text x='260' y='90' class='sub ink'>Playback Singers</text><text x='200' y='115' class='sub ink'>Film Stars</text><text x='148' y='132' class='sub ink' font-size='12'>Directors</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three nested circles: outermost is Playback Singers, middle is Film Stars, innermost is Film Directors, showing the subset chain.

given.aim: Determine whether each of the two conclusions follows necessarily from the given statements

terms.term: Statement I: All film stars are playback singers
terms.meaning: Film Stars is a subset of Playback Singers (FS subset PS).
terms.example: Every person who is a film star is also a playback singer.
terms.connects: Provides the outer layer of the subset chain.

terms.term: Statement II: All film directors are film stars
terms.meaning: Film Directors is a subset of Film Stars (FD subset FS).
terms.example: Every film director is necessarily a film star.
terms.connects: Provides the inner layer: FD subset FS subset PS, enabling transitivity.

terms.term: Conclusion I: All film directors are playback singers
terms.meaning: FD subset PS, which follows from FD subset FS and FS subset PS.
terms.example: Since directors are stars and stars are singers, directors are singers.
terms.connects: Direct application of transitive closure of subset relation.

given.plan: Use transitivity of the universal affirmative: All S are M AND All M are P implies All S are P. Also, 'All A are B' with a non-empty domain implies 'Some B are A'.

steps.title: Check Conclusion I: All film directors are playback singers
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (B -> C), conclude (A -> C)
steps.apply: Statement II: Director(x) -> FilmStar(x). Statement I: FilmStar(x) -> Singer(x). By hypothetical syllogism: Director(x) -> Singer(x). So All film directors are playback singers. Conclusion I FOLLOWS.
steps.note: This is the Barbara syllogism form (AAA-1), the most fundamental valid syllogism.

steps.title: Check Conclusion II: Some film stars are film directors
steps.formula_raw: 'All A have property B' = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: Statement II says: All film directors are film stars. This means every element of the director set is also in the film star set. Since the director set is a non-empty subset of film stars, we can say: Some film stars are (specifically those who are) directors. Therefore 'Some film stars are film directors' FOLLOWS.
steps.note: When 'All A are B' and A is non-empty, it follows that 'Some B are A' (converse particular).

steps.title: State the combined result
steps.formula_raw: Statement R implies C when every model making R true also makes C true
steps.apply: Both Conclusion I (transitivity of subset) and Conclusion II (particular from universal) follow from the given statements. Therefore option D is correct.
steps.note: Both conclusions are logically entailed by the premises in every possible model where the statements are true.

solution.result: D. Both conclusions I and II follow.

questions.formula_note: The Barbara syllogism (All M are P, All S are M => All S are P) yields Conclusion I by transitivity; Conclusion II follows from the converse particular of Statement II (All directors are film stars implies some film stars are directors).

questions.question:
1. Tanya is older than Eric.
2. Cliff is older than Tanya.
3. Eric is older than Cliff.

If the first two statements are true, then the third statement is:

A. True
B. False
C. Uncertain
D. Data insufficient
questions.to_find: The truth value of the third statement given that the first two statements are true

understand.plain: If Tanya is older than Eric (T > E) and Cliff is older than Tanya (C > T), then by transitivity of the 'older than' relation we get C > T > E, meaning Cliff is the oldest and Eric is the youngest. The third statement says Eric is older than Cliff (E > C), which directly contradicts C > E. So the third statement must be False whenever the first two are true.

keywords.explain: If a > b and b > c, then a > c. The 'older than' relation is transitive.
keywords.example: Cliff > Tanya and Tanya > Eric implies Cliff > Eric by transitivity.

keywords.explain: The 'older than' (strictly greater) relation on ages is irreflexive, asymmetric, and transitive.
keywords.example: If Cliff > Eric, then Eric > Cliff cannot be true (asymmetry).

keywords.explain: Two statements that cannot both be true simultaneously; one must be false if the other is true.
keywords.example: 'Cliff > Eric' derived from premises directly contradicts 'Eric > Cliff' stated in Statement 3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='340' height='160' viewBox='0 0 340 160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='160' rx='12' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><rect x='20' y='55' width='80' height='36' rx='8' class='hi'/><rect x='130' y='55' width='80' height='36' rx='8' class='hi'/><rect x='240' y='55' width='80' height='36' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='60' y='79' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Cliff</text><text x='170' y='79' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Tanya</text><text x='280' y='79' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Eric</text><line x1='100' y1='73' x2='130' y2='73' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2.5' marker-end='url(#a)'/><line x1='210' y1='73' x2='240' y2='73' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2.5' marker-end='url(#a)'/><text x='108' y='65' class='sub mut'>></text><text x='218' y='65' class='sub mut'>></text><text x='170' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>Stmt 3: Eric > Cliff is FALSE</text><defs><marker id='a' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Number line showing Cliff is oldest, then Tanya, then Eric youngest, with Statement 3 (Eric > Cliff) marked as False.

given.aim: Determine whether Statement 3 (Eric > Cliff) is true, false, or uncertain when Statements 1 and 2 are both true

terms.meaning: Tanya is strictly older than Eric.
terms.example: If Tanya is 25 and Eric is 20, this is satisfied.
terms.connects: Establishes T > E, giving a partial order between Tanya and Eric.

terms.meaning: Cliff is strictly older than Tanya.
terms.example: If Cliff is 30, Tanya 25, this is satisfied.
terms.connects: Combined with Statement 1 via transitivity: Cliff > Tanya > Eric, so Cliff > Eric.

terms.meaning: Eric is strictly older than Cliff - the claim to evaluate.
terms.example: This would require Eric to be older than Cliff.
terms.connects: Since from premises we derived Cliff > Eric, this statement is the direct negation and must be False.

given.plan: Derive the ordering implied by Statements 1 and 2 using transitivity, then check whether Statement 3 is consistent or contradictory with this ordering.

steps.title: Establish ordering from the two true statements
steps.formula_raw: transitive: forall x forall y forall z (R(x,y) AND R(y,z) -> R(x,z))
steps.apply: Statement 1: Tanya > Eric (T > E). Statement 2: Cliff > Tanya (C > T). By transitivity of 'older than': C > T and T > E => C > E. So the full ordering is: Cliff > Tanya > Eric.
steps.note: The 'older than' relation is strictly transitive, so we can chain inequalities.

steps.title: Compare Statement 3 against the derived ordering
steps.formula_raw: (A -> B) is logically equivalent to (not B -> not A)
steps.apply: From the premises we derived C > E (Cliff is older than Eric). Statement 3 claims E > C (Eric is older than Cliff). Since 'older than' is asymmetric (C > E implies NOT E > C), Statement 3 directly contradicts the derived fact. Therefore Statement 3 is FALSE.
steps.note: Asymmetry: if a > b is true then b > a is necessarily false.

steps.title: Confirm it is not uncertain or insufficient
steps.formula_raw: p -> q is False only when p is True and q is False
steps.apply: There is no ambiguity: the premises uniquely determine C > T > E, which makes E > C impossible. The answer is definitively False, not uncertain or data insufficient.
steps.note: C (Uncertain) would apply only if the premises were compatible with Statement 3 being either true or false - they are not, since Statement 3 is always false given the premises.

questions.formula_note: Transitivity of 'older than' chains C > T and T > E to give C > E; asymmetry then makes E > C (Statement 3) necessarily false, so the answer is B. False.
